title: Basic Optical Aids: Mirrors, Magnifiers, and Light Sources
narrationScript:
Visual testing starts with your eye, but the eye has limits. Direct visual examination generally wants your eye within twenty-four inches of the surface, at an angle of at least thirty degrees to it. Real hardware rarely cooperates. Welds hide behind flanges. Threads sit at the bottom of blind holes. The finest surface cracks sit right at the limit of what the eye can resolve. That is where basic optical aids come in. In this lesson we cover three families: mirrors, magnifiers, and lighting.

Start with mirrors, because they are the cheapest and most used aid in the kit. A mirror obeys one rule. The angle the light comes in equals the angle it leaves, measured from a line perpendicular to the mirror face. Set the mirror at forty-five degrees and your line of sight turns a clean ninety-degree corner. Flat mirrors show true size and shape, and that is what you want for most examination work. A convex mirror shows a wider area, but everything looks smaller. Use it to scan the inside of a large cavity, then come back with a flat mirror for detail. Quality matters too. A standard mirror has its coating behind the glass, so light also bounces off the front of the glass and you get a faint double image. A first-surface mirror has the coating on the front. No ghost image and a crisper view, but that coating scratches easily, so treat it gently. Inspection mirrors come on telescoping handles with swivel heads, in sizes from a fingernail to a postcard, and many carry their own LED ring. One habit to build early: in a mirror, everything you do with your hands appears reversed. Practice steering a pointer in the mirror before you rely on it for real work.

Next, magnifiers. The power of a simple magnifier is roughly two hundred fifty millimeters divided by the focal length of the lens. A lens with a fifty millimeter focal length gives about five power. Twenty-five millimeters gives about ten power. Here is the catch. Every step up in power shrinks the field of view, shortens the working distance, and thins the depth of field. So work in two stages. Find with low power, around one and a half to three power, scanning a wide field. Evaluate with high power, five to ten power, once you have found something. A ten-power loupe is the standard tool for judging fine surface detail. Better loupes use doublet or triplet lens designs, which keep the image sharp and the colors true all the way to the edge. A measuring magnifier adds a small printed scale at its focus, graduated in tenths of a millimeter, so you can size a small indication directly.

Now lighting, which makes or breaks every visual test. Codes typically require about five hundred lux on the surface for general examination, and about one thousand lux for critical or direct examination. You do not estimate that. You verify it with a calibrated light meter held at the inspection surface, and you record the reading. One footcandle is about ten point seven six lux, if your procedure speaks in footcandles. Today the workhorse source is the LED. It runs cool, survives drops, lasts for years, and lights instantly. One caution. If your call depends on color, like heat tint or corrosion products, check that the lamp renders color well. Older incandescent lamps were the gold standard for color rendering. Cheap LEDs are not always as good.

Beyond raw brightness, how you aim the light is a technique of its own. Light skimming across a surface at a low angle throws shadows from the smallest bumps and valleys. That grazing light reveals shallow dents, undercut, and tight surface cracks that flat lighting hides. Diffuse light does the opposite. It softens the beam to kill glare on shiny machined parts. Backlighting silhouettes an edge or shines through a hole. A polarizing filter strips the glare off wet or glossy surfaces.

Finally, care. These are precision instruments, not toolbox filler. Blow grit off a lens before you wipe it, and use lens tissue, not a shop rag. Retire scratched mirrors and lenses, because scratches scatter light and can hide or mimic indications. Verify before use. Read fine print through the mirror or magnifier. Check a measuring reticle against a calibrated scale. Keep the light meter in calibration, because that meter is your proof the job met the procedure. Take care of the small stuff, and it will take care of your inspection.

sections.heading: Why Optical Aids Earn Their Place
sections.body:
Direct visual testing has a geometry requirement that shows up in code after code: get the eye within 24 in. (600 mm) of the surface, at a viewing angle of not less than 30 degrees to that surface. Real hardware fights you on both counts. Welds hide behind flanges, threads sit at the bottom of counterbores, and the finest surface cracks sit at the edge of what a healthy eye can resolve — about one arc-minute for 20/20 vision. Optical aids exist to win back that geometry and that resolution.

This lesson covers the three families you will carry on almost every VT job:

- **Mirrors** redirect the line of sight around obstructions.
- **Magnifiers** enlarge detail the unaided eye cannot resolve.
- **Light sources and special lighting** put enough light — and the right kind of light — on the surface.

None of them help if they are scratched, dirty, or unverified, so we close with the care and verification habits that keep these simple tools delivering code-quality examinations.

sections.heading: Inspection Mirrors
sections.body:
A mirror obeys the law of reflection: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal — the imaginary line perpendicular to the mirror face. Set a mirror at 45 degrees and a horizontal line of sight turns a clean 90-degree corner. That one fact lets you examine the back side of a flange, the far wall of a nozzle, or the root side of a weld without moving the part.

Know your mirror types:

- **Flat mirrors** show true size and shape — the default for examination.
- **Convex mirrors** widen the field of view but shrink the image. Scan a large cavity with one, then return with a flat mirror for detail.
- **Concave mirrors** magnify at close range; they appear in a few specialty heads but are the least common.

Construction matters. A second-surface mirror carries its reflective coating behind the glass, where it is protected — but the front of the glass also reflects, producing a faint ghost image that can blur a fine scribe line or crack. A first-surface mirror coats the front of the glass: one crisp image, no ghost, at the cost of a delicate coating. Hardware runs from 13 mm round heads to roughly 75 x 125 mm rectangles, on telescoping handles with swivel or ball joints, and many heads now carry built-in LED rings. Build the habit early: mirror images reverse your hand motions, so practice steering a pick or pointer under mirror view before you depend on it.

sections.caption: A flat mirror set at 45 degrees turns the line of sight 90 degrees to reach a weld hidden behind an obstruction, and a first-surface coating avoids the ghost image a second-surface mirror adds.

sections.heading: Magnifiers: Power, Field of View, and Working Distance
sections.body:
The approximate power of a simple magnifier is 250 mm divided by its focal length, because 250 mm is the standard near-point distance of the relaxed eye. A 125 mm lens gives about 2x, a 50 mm lens about 5x, and a 25 mm lens about 10x. Power always costs you three things:

- **Field of view** shrinks — a 2x reader covers most of a weld; a 10x loupe covers about 20 mm.
- **Working distance** shrinks — roughly the focal length, so about 25 mm for a 10x loupe.
- **Depth of field** shrinks, so curved surfaces drift in and out of focus.

So inspect in two stages: locate with low power, 1.5x to 3x, scanning a wide field; evaluate with higher power, 5x to 10x, once you have found something. A 10x loupe is the accepted workhorse for judging fine surface detail. Quality loupes use cemented doublet or triplet designs — the Hastings triplet is the classic — to hold sharpness and true color out to the lens edge; a cheap single lens distorts and color-fringes exactly where you are trying to look. Two specialty tools earn space in the kit: the **measuring magnifier**, typically 7x to 10x with a reticle graduated in 0.1 mm divisions held at a fixed focus, lets you size a small indication directly; **illuminated stand and headband magnifiers** at 1.5x to 3.5x keep both hands free for bench work.

sections.title: The 250-Millimeter Rule
sections.body: **Power = 250 mm / focal length.** Shorter focal length means more power — and less field of view, less working distance, and less depth of field. Choose the **lowest** power that resolves the detail you must evaluate; jumping straight to 10x makes scanning slow and unreliable.

sections.caption: Stepping from 2x to 10x enlarges the detail but shrinks both the field of view and the working distance — locate at low power, evaluate at high power.

sections.heading: Light Sources for Visual Testing
sections.body:
Detection rises and falls with light on the surface. Codes commonly set two floors: about **500 lux (50 fc)** for general examination and about **1000 lux (100 fc)** for critical or direct examination — measured at the inspection surface, not at the lamp. One footcandle is about 10.76 lux. You demonstrate compliance with a calibrated light meter held where the inspection happens, and you record the reading; an expired meter calibration can void an otherwise good exam.

Know the source families:

- **Incandescent and halogen** — continuous spectrum and excellent color rendering, but hot, fragile, and short-lived.
- **Fluorescent** — diffuse, even, cool light for benches and booths.
- **LED** — rugged, cool, instant-on, efficient, and now the field standard for flashlights and area lighting. Check the color rendering index (CRI of 80 or better is a sensible floor) when a call depends on color, such as heat tint, oxide colors, or corrosion products.
- **Fiber-optic light guides** — pipe cool light from a remote source into cavities and around optics.

Color temperature changes how surfaces look: a warm 2700 K lamp and a 6500 K daylight LED render the same heat-tint band differently, so keep the source consistent across comparisons. And carry spare batteries — a dimming flashlight quietly drops you below the code minimum.

sections.caption: Special lighting techniques and what each one reveals.
rows: Single source skimming the surface at a low angle, roughly 5-30 degrees
Shadows from shallow relief: dents, undercut, weld toe profile, tight surface cracks
Source softened through a diffuser or bounced off a white surface
Glare-free view of shiny machined or polished surfaces; uniform field
Source behind the part or aimed through an opening
Silhouetted edges, burrs, through-holes, hole alignment
Polarizing filter over the source, the eye, or both
Cuts specular glare on wet, oily, or glossy surfaces
Fluorescent residues and leak-tracer dyes; the basis of fluorescent PT/MT viewing
Flash rate synchronized to part rotation
Apparent freeze of rotating or vibrating parts for examination

sections.heading: Care and Verification of Optical Aids
sections.body:
Treat optical aids as precision instruments, not toolbox filler. Blow grit off lenses and mirrors before wiping — dragging a dry rag across embedded grit is how scratches happen — then clean with lens tissue and an approved cleaner. Retire scratched mirrors and lenses: scratches scatter light, kill contrast, and can hide or mimic a linear indication. Keep first-surface mirrors in cases and away from solvents that attack the coating.

Verify before use, every shift:

- **Mirrors and magnifiers** — read fine print or a resolution card through the aid at working distance; the image should be crisp, undistorted, and free of ghosting.
- **Measuring reticles** — check the reticle against a calibrated reference scale before sizing anything reportable.
- **Flashlights and area lights** — confirm intensity at the surface with the light meter; weak batteries fail quietly.
- **Light meter** — confirm the calibration sticker is current (typically a one-year interval) before you sign a report that depends on its reading.

When the procedure requires it, record the aids used — for example the magnifier power and the measured lux at the surface — so the examination can be reproduced later.

figures.title: Magnification vs Field of View and Working Distance

checkQuestions.question: An inspector sees a faint double image of a fine scribe line when using a pocket inspection mirror. What is the most likely cause?
options: The mirror is convex rather than flat
It is a second-surface mirror, so the front of the glass adds a ghost reflection
The viewing angle is less than 30 degrees
The LED ring is too bright
checkQuestions.explanation: A second-surface mirror reflects from both the coating behind the glass and the glass front itself, producing a faint ghost image. A first-surface mirror puts the coating in front and gives one crisp image.

checkQuestions.question: A simple magnifier has a focal length of 50 mm. Its approximate power is:
options: 2x
5x
checkQuestions.explanation: Power is approximately 250 mm divided by the focal length: 250 / 50 = 5x.

checkQuestions.question: As magnifier power increases, what happens to the field of view and the working distance?
options: Both decrease
Both increase
Field of view increases while working distance decreases
Field of view decreases while working distance increases
checkQuestions.explanation: Higher power means a shorter focal length, which shrinks the field of view, the working distance, and the depth of field all at once — the core trade-off of magnification.

checkQuestions.question: A procedure requires 1000 lux for a critical weld examination. The correct way to demonstrate compliance is to:
options: Use a flashlight rated at 1000 lumens or more
Estimate from the lamp wattage and distance
Rely on the shop lighting survey done last year
Measure with a calibrated light meter at the inspection surface and record the reading
checkQuestions.explanation: Illumination requirements apply at the surface being examined, so the only acceptable proof is a reading from a calibrated meter held at that surface, documented in the record.

checkQuestions.question: Which lighting setup best reveals a shallow dent or fine surface relief on a flat plate?
options: Diffuse light arriving from all directions
A single source at a low, grazing angle to the surface
Backlighting from behind the plate
Maximum intensity aimed perpendicular to the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Oblique or grazing light makes even shallow relief cast shadows, which is exactly what reveals dents, undercut, and tight surface cracks. Diffuse and perpendicular lighting flatten that relief.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Topical Outline, Level I, 4.1 Mirrors, 4.2 Magnifiers, 4.7 Light sources and special lighting
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 9 (illumination levels and direct-viewing practice, referenced generically)
Employer written practice per SNT-TC-1A / NAS410 (equipment verification and documentation requirements)
